summary: Al-insan al-kamil, 'the Perfect Human', is a doctrine in the speculative Sufi tradition, especially the school of Ibn Arabi and its later elaboration by al-Jili, according to which the fully realized human being is the microcosm that comprehensively reflects the divine names and serves as the link between God and the cosmos. It is associated in the tradition with the reality of the prophets and of Muhammad in particular.
description:
In the metaphysics associated with Ibn Arabi (d. 1240), the Perfect Human (al-insan al-kamil) is the human being who realizes in himself the totality of the divine names and thereby mirrors the whole of reality: a microcosm answering to the macrocosm, and the isthmus or link (barzakh) through which God is known to the cosmos and the cosmos is related to God. In this teaching the human form alone is created upon the comprehensive image encompassing all the divine names, so that the perfected human is the locus in which God contemplates His own self-disclosure; the tradition identifies this reality above all with the prophets and, at its summit, with the reality of Muhammad (al-haqiqa al-muhammadiyya).

The doctrine received its most sustained treatment from Abd al-Karim al-Jili (d. disputed, reported c. 1408-1428) in his work al-Insan al-Kamil, which systematized and named the theme drawn from Ibn Arabi and his commentators. Al-Jili sets out the Perfect Human as the cosmic archetype and the point at which the divine and created orders meet, and relates it to the divine names and to the hierarchy of being. Modern scholarship, from Nicholson's early study onward, has traced al-Jili's development of the idea and his debt to Ibn Arabi. Because the doctrine belongs to the same speculative Sufi current as wahdat al-wujud, it participates in the wider contestation of that tradition; this entry reports it as a doctrine held within that school and does not adjudicate the disputes surrounding it.
editorial_notes: Scope: al-insan al-kamil as a doctrine of the speculative Sufi (Akbarian) tradition, from Ibn Arabi to al-Jili. Presented descriptively and attributed, not in a devotional register, per neutrality principles #2, #4 and #7; the identification of the reality with the prophets and with the 'Muhammadan reality' (al-haqiqa al-muhammadiyya) is reported as the tradition's own claim, not asserted as fact. Because the doctrine sits within the same current as wahdat al-wujud, the entry notes that it shares in that current's contestation without re-litigating it here. al-Jili's death date is disputed and given as a range: born c. 767/1365, died c. 811-832 AH / c. 1408-1428 CE. Peer-review fix (v0.1.0 promotion pass, 2026-07-19): the earlier single death year of c. 1408 was too early and too precise; the attested range runs later, with 826/1422 and 832/1428 both reported by contemporaries and reference works commonly giving c. 1424, so the date is widened to a disputed range (reported c. 1408-1428) here and in the summary and relation notes, and historical_period.end is set to a representative later value (1424). Relations: articulated_by ibn-arabi and al-jili (al-jili is a dangling target, no entry yet, which is permitted), part_of sufism, related_to wahdat-al-wujud and tawhid. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern Western academic study (Nicholson, Studies in Islamic Mysticism, whose chapter on al-Jili treats the Insan al-Kamil, web-verified); (b) the primary text, al-Jili's al-Insan al-Kamil, and Ibn Arabi's Fusus behind it; (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (Chittick on Ibn Arabi's metaphysics). Verified tier (2026-07-19): the entry's load-bearing claims were confirmed against Nicholson's Studies in Islamic Mysticism, chapter II ('The Perfect Man', pp. 77-142), read in full from the Internet Archive djvu text. Confirmed: (1) the Perfect Man as microcosm reflecting the divine names/powers as in a mirror ('The characteristic of the Insanu 'l-kamil is the idea of the Perfect Man, who as a microcosmos of a higher order reflects not only the powers of nature but also the divine powers as in a mirror', p. 104; 'Man is the microcosm in which all attributes are united, and in him alone does the Absolute become conscious of itself', p. 106), and as the link between God and the cosmos ('Man, in virtue of his essence, is the cosmic Thought assuming flesh and connecting Absolute Being with the world of Nature', p. 106; the barzakh/intermediate register appears in the same chapter); (2) the identification of this reality with the prophets and above all with Muhammad / al-Haqiqa al-Muhammadiyya ('The name Mohammed is not applied except to the Idea of Mohammed (al-Haqiqatu 'l-Muhammadiyya)', p. 105; the Muhammadan Reality speaks in the first person at p. 113; 'the Illumination of the Absolute is given to the Heavenly Man (Mohammed) alone and transmitted through him to the Perfect Men who are his representatives on earth', p. 130); (3) al-Jili's debt to Ibn Arabi (Ibn Arabi, 'whose influence is manifest in every page that Jili wrote', preface p. viii; 'On this basis Jili builds his mystical philosophy', p. 104; 'Jili belongs to the school of Sufis who hold that Being is one', p. 104). Nicholson (the carrying source) is therefore raised to high confidence. The other two sources remain medium and were NOT read line-by-line: al-Jili's own al-Insan al-Kamil (Arabic primary text, not accessed here) and Chittick's The Sufi Path of Knowledge (consulted only bibliographically); they are corroborating, not load-bearing, since Nicholson carries the claims.

relations.note: The doctrine of the Perfect Human as the comprehensive locus of the divine names is central to Ibn Arabi's metaphysics.
Abd al-Karim al-Jili (d. disputed, reported c. 1408-1428) gave the theme its most sustained and systematic treatment in his al-Insan al-Kamil (dangling target).
A doctrine of the speculative, metaphysical current of Sufism after Ibn Arabi.
Belongs to the same Akbarian metaphysics as wahdat al-wujud and shares in its contested reception; the disputes are noted, not adjudicated.
The Perfect Human is presented as the locus in which the unity of the divine names (and so tawhid) is comprehensively realized, in a metaphysical register.

historical_period.note: Developed within Ibn Arabi's school from the 7th/13th century and given its classic systematic form by al-Jili. His death date is disputed (reported c. 1408-1428, with 826/1422 and 832/1428 both attested and reference works commonly giving c. 1424); end year is a representative later value. Dates approximate.

sources.citation: Nicholson, Reynold A. Studies in Islamic Mysticism. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1921. The study of al-Jili (chapter II, 'The Perfect Man', pp. 77-142) elucidates the mystical philosophy of the Insan al-Kamil (the Perfect Man) and traces al-Jili's debt to Ibn Arabi. Full text read on the Internet Archive.

al-Jili, Abd al-Karim. al-Insan al-Kamil fi ma'rifat al-awakhir wa-l-awa'il (The Perfect Human in the Knowledge of Things Last and First), early 9th/15th c. Classical primary source: the systematic Sufi treatment of the Perfect Human doctrine. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Public-domain classical Arabic text; cited for reference.

sources.citation: Chittick, William C. The Sufi Path of Knowledge: Ibn al-'Arabi's Metaphysics of Imagination. Albany, NY: State University of New York Press, 1989. A contemporary Muslim scholar's study of Ibn Arabi's metaphysics, including the Perfect Human as the comprehensive locus of the divine names. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.
